column15:
Many classic games were never built with mod support, yet communities still find ways to extend them. This talk explores a “shim” approach: building a replacement entry point that stands in for the original executable and calls into the same engine functions the game already uses. By treating the engine like an API, we create a structured, reliable pathway for customization without rewriting the engine itself.

With that adapter layer in place, the asset pipeline can be redirected to load content the game normally wouldn’t accept—custom textures, scripts, and optional plugin modules (DLL-style add-ons)—through a clean, modular loader. The technical focus is designing a stable mod-loading pipeline: clear configuration, asset discovery and validation, safe load order, and strong logging so mods can be enabled, disabled, and debugged with confidence.

It’s a strong “Learn by Doing / FAIL” STEM topic because the most valuable lessons come from what breaks: crashes from mismatched function signatures, memory ownership mistakes, initialization timing issues, and surprising asset-format constraints. Each failure becomes a practical debugging exercise that teaches systems programming fundamentals, software architecture decisions, and tooling habits. The project is framed responsibly around interoperability and experimentation—extending software behavior in a controlled way—rather than bypassing protections or encouraging misuse.

column15:
I participated in a UCB Summer Research Program last summer (C-GEM SURP) and would like to give a short (~10min) talk at the Science Fair. My talk would be similar to the talks given last March by former FUURI participants: I would discuss my research project, my experience, and advice for fellow students who also wish to join such a summer program. Generally, my research project was in the field of chemical biology with a focus on peptide chemistry. 
Here is the abstract for my project:
"Macrocyclic peptides are highly valuable as a drug modality due to their size and rigidity, providing these scaffolds the ability to target “undruggable targets”. Recent work from the Schepartz lab has demonstrated a novel way to additionally rigidify macrocyclic peptide conformations via a modified Friedländer reaction that installs a heterocyclic quinoline motif directly in the peptide backbone, producing a high rotational barrier around the adjacent chiral aryl-amide atropisomeric axis. Macrocyclization of a singular linear precursor generated a pair of conformationally distinct atropisomeric products with a distinct 3:1 diastereomeric product ratio. The observed M:P ratio could be leveraged to synthesize the desired stereoisomer necessary for a specific drug target. This work aims to elucidate the design rules for such peptides in three aspects: identifying the relationship between ring size and rotational barrier, elucidating the impact of peptide sequence on product distribution, and finally, improving on the solid-phase synthesis of these macrocycles by adapting new synthetic strategies. Expanding the design rules of these atropisomeric Friedländer macrocycles should lay the groundwork for the rational design of libraries of atropisomeric macrocycles to screen for peptides able to target “undruggable targets” and provide a guideline for the selective synthesis of more complex atropisomeric macrocyclic peptides."

column15:
In the simplest terms, it's a mod loader for a video-game.
Many games have mods that allow the community to make a wide range of modifications to the game. For older games mods allows the community to bring life back into the game through things such as HD Textures, Bug-Fixes, 3D Model Reworks and much more.

For many games it's common place to split the program across multiple library's, usually an game.exe, engine, filesystem and game.dll. These components all communicate through functions exported from each other.
The game.exe is usually simple, just initializing the core components of the engine,game and filesystem.

Dying Light (2015) is a parkour survival horror game developed by Techland, This talk shows how by reverse engineering the various library's exported functions and hooking specific functions we can build a replacement executable that mimics the functionality of the original while allowing us to implement proper mod loading.
column16:
Programing knowledge, in this case C++. You would need to understand how functions are exported, how they are imported, matching the exported function's mangled signatures and how to link to a existing library without it's source code.
Reverse engineering through programs like IDA and Ghidra to get an understanding of how the original program works.

Besides just generally figuring out how the game starts and loads everything, there's also the best way to load mods, how to overwrite assets and load other user created library's.
column21: Yes

column15: A social event app called MuseOn assists students, athletes, and artists in forming and hosting semi-spontaneous events in their community. The app's central theme is music and its ability to unite; hosts create a plan (study group, pickup sports, art exposition), set a location (verify initial condition if a public space), and a playlist, then publish their post on the app, which notifies members within a set radius. Passerby's can also join by scanning a QR code or receiving a Bluetooth invitation. Upon joining the "bubble" members can link their music preferences through the app, which uses an algorithm based on the surrounding environment, set goals, and member preferences to pick the next song. Hosts are responsible for maintaining order at events and are assisted by the app. Anyone walking by who deems the music too loud or notices other unruly behaviors can report it through the same QR/Bluetooth system. In the case of a report, hosts must verify the condition of the public space through photos or a video. Hosts then receive a Mu score based on these inputs and can credit participants for any positive contributions, creating a system that strongly reinforces positive behavior. Ultimately, connections are formed between interest groups that otherwise would have never interacted, and members are incentivized to spend more time outside their comfort zone while still maintaining peace and safety for them and the surrounding community.

This project is a mod loader for the game Dying Light (2015), The goal is to load mods such as custom resource packs (models/textures), custom material packs (shaders/material data) and DLL's (library's that contain additional code).

The project works by replacing the original game executable with my custom executable, this is possible because of the way the game was programed, instead of having one program that contains the entire game it's instead split up into multiple parts (the engine, game, filesystem, renderer and then DyingLightGame.exe)
the game executable is pretty simple, calling only a few needed functions from the engine and filesystem in order to start the game and load assets, Once re-implemented my custom executable can load extra data the original game would not have intended.

column15:
The FalconEye project is a high-performance, local AI assistant inspired by the seamless, intuitive interface of Ironman's J.A.R.V.I.S. Much like Tony Stark’s legendary companion, FalconEye aims to bridge the gap between human interaction and machine intelligence, through an on-device AI personal assistant that helpfully reacts to the user requests.

**The Vision**
At its core, FalconEye is built for the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano Super, turning this compact edge AI hardware into a digital assistant. It is designed to be "always-on" and "always-aware," processing voice and visual data locally to ensure maximum privacy, persistent memory and minimal latency.

**Technical Solution**
By utilizing advanced Small Language Models (SLMs) and optimized speech-text models, FalconEye achieves a level of responsiveness and intelligent reasoning that mirrors Ironman's J.A.R.V.I.S. To enable human-machine interaction, our project integrates a real-time visual overlay of information curated by the AI assistant. To achieve this product within a compact hardware footprint, the project focuses on extreme resource optimization. Every component (AI and software) is tuned to live within the device's unified memory, balancing complex neural networks with a fluid, high-frame-rate graphical interface. The result is a sophisticated, "on-device" AI that demonstrates the power of modern edge computing: a portable, private, and powerful digital assistant.
column16: Python and JavaScript programming, Speech-Text and Small Language Models, Jetson Orin (Linux), Software Engineering

column15:
The E-Donuts Engineering Club is proud to present two student-engineered musical instruments: a custom Wind Synthesizer and a handcrafted Hurdy-Gurdy. This project explores how engineering principles can transform both digital systems and mechanical structures into expressive musical tools.

Our Wind Synthesizer is an electronic instrument that responds to breath pressure and finger inputs, much like a traditional wind instrument. Using pressure sensors, touch controls, and a microcontroller, the system converts breath intensity and fingering patterns into digital signals that generate sound. Students designed and programmed the instrument to demonstrate concepts such as signal processing, analog-to-digital conversion, waveform synthesis, and embedded systems engineering.

In contrast, the Hurdy-Gurdy showcases mechanical engineering at its finest. This centuries-old string instrument produces sound using a rotating wheel that continuously vibrates the strings, functioning like a mechanical bow. Students engineered the crank mechanism, string tension system, and keybox structure to precisely control pitch and resonance. Through this build, members applied principles of vibration, frequency, resonance, friction, and structural design.

By building them from the ground up, students gained hands-on experience in design iteration, prototyping, fabrication, programming, acoustics, and systems integration.

This project demonstrates how engineering is not limited to circuits or code, it can also be heard, felt, and performed. Through creativity and technical skill, E-Donuts members worked hard on these projects and will be excited to showcase them.

column13: Investigating the Role of Fungal Enzymes in Degrading Environmental Phthalates
column15:
This project is part of an ongoing research project at FLC and UC Davis. The focus of the project is to identify specific enzymes created by the fungal species, Ganoderma lucidum, which have the potential to metabolize phthalates in the environment and ultimately use analytical chemistry to quantify this capacity. 

Folsom Lake College (FLC) is home to a protected wetland ecosystem that is part of the historic Natoma Ditch. This area along the North East side of the campus property was set aside as part of an environmental mitigation plan during an expansion project at the FLC- El Dorado Center in Placerville. The wetland area has cultural significance for the local Nisenan Maidu and Miwok communities. The aim of this project and future research is to integrate the wetland as key component of authentic learning experiences. In order to accomplish this goal we needed to develop methods that are applicable to a variety of classes. Analyzing water quality and collecting data for biomonitoring helped to inform mycoremediation research at Folsom Lake College. 

Long term, this research interest is to determine what role native species of fungi play in the degradation of environmental contaminants such as phthalates found in mass manufactured products. Employing new and innovative technologies like nanopore sequencing has allowed a deeper level of inquiry for research internships such as this one.

column15:
Project Northstar: Peer-to-Peer Live Streaming Without a CDN

Project Northstar is a self-hostable live streaming platform that demonstrates how modern peer-to-peer networking can fundamentally change how video streaming systems are built. Traditional streaming platforms such as Twitch or YouTube rely heavily on centralized Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) that distribute video data from large data centers to viewers around the world. While effective, this architecture is extremely expensive, energy intensive, and difficult for independent creators or small communities to replicate.

Northstar explores an alternative architecture: decentralized peer-to-peer live streaming using WebRTC. Instead of routing all video traffic through a central server, the system allows viewers to receive the stream directly from the broadcaster through encrypted peer-to-peer connections. A lightweight Node.js signaling server coordinates the initial connection process using WebSockets, but once connections are established, the video stream flows directly between participants. This dramatically reduces infrastructure requirements while maintaining high-quality real-time streaming.

The system is built using modern web technologies including WebRTC, WebSockets, JavaScript, HTML5, and Node.js. The platform supports real-time screen broadcasting, live chat, selectable streaming quality presets (720p or 1080p), and codec preference options such as VP9 or H.264 to balance video clarity and device compatibility. The interface is designed to be simple and accessible while still demonstrating advanced networking concepts under the hood.

Northstar also demonstrates practical solutions to challenges commonly encountered in real-time streaming systems. The platform includes role-aware signaling for streamers and viewers, automatic reconnection logic, and dynamic peer connection management that allows multiple viewers to join and leave a stream without interrupting the broadcast. The system can operate locally over a LAN or be exposed to the internet through secure HTTPS connections.

Beyond demonstrating a working streaming platform, this project highlights broader themes in computer science and network engineering. Peer-to-peer architectures can reduce infrastructure costs, lower latency, and distribute network load more efficiently than traditional centralized systems. These ideas have potential applications in future communication platforms, decentralized media systems, and community-driven content networks.

Northstar will be demonstrated live across multiple devices to show how a single broadcaster can stream video directly to multiple viewers using peer-to-peer connections in real time. The project illustrates how modern browser technologies enable powerful distributed systems to run entirely on standard consumer hardware without requiring large server infrastructure.

See my GitHub here: https://github.com/RxFaction/Northstar-Mainframe
column16:
Further development of Project Northstar would benefit from software engineers with experience in JavaScript and Node.js, particularly those familiar with real-time networking technologies such as WebSockets and WebRTC. Knowledge of distributed systems and peer-to-peer networking architectures would also be valuable, as the platform explores decentralized approaches to live streaming rather than traditional server-based infrastructure.

Additional skills in front-end web development (HTML5, responsive UI design), networking concepts such as NAT traversal and signaling, and media encoding/streaming protocols would help expand the platform’s capabilities and improve performance across different devices and network environments.

From a resource standpoint, the project primarily requires standard consumer hardware such as personal computers, mobile devices, and a stable internet connection to test peer-to-peer streaming across multiple endpoints. Development tools include Node.js, modern web browsers with WebRTC support, and open-source libraries for networking and media handling.

In the long term, Northstar aims to explore alternatives to existing WebRTC implementations by researching and potentially developing custom and proprietary peer-to-peer streaming infrastructure tailored specifically for decentralized live media distribution.

column13: Analysis of US Car Accident Statistics
column15: Our project focuses on developing an AI-driven system that analyzes datasets from Kaggle and transforms complex information into clear, accessible insights. The goal is to bridge the gap between raw data and meaningful understanding by automating data exploration and visualization in a way that is both efficient and user-friendly.  At its core, the model ingests structured datasets and applies a combination of machine learning techniques and statistical analysis to identify patterns, trends, and key relationships within the data. Instead of requiring users to manually clean, process, and interpret the dataset, the system streamlines this workflow by handling these steps automatically. This allows users to quickly gain valuable insights without needing advanced data science expertise.  One of the key features of the project is its ability to generate intuitive visual outputs. The AI produces a range of graphs, tables, and summary statistics that present the data in a clear and digestible format. These visualizations are designed to highlight important findings, such as correlations, distributions, and anomalies, making it easier for users to understand and communicate results. Overall, this project aims to make data analysis more accessible, efficient, and insightful by combining AI automation with clear visual communication, enabling users to turn complex datasets into actionable knowledge.

column13: Price vs. Popularity: Understanding Market Trends on Steam
column15: This project explores the relationship between the pricing of video games and popularity on the Steam platform. With the assistance of a large, in-depth dataset from Kaggle, we seek to understand if game price influences player engagement or sales. Specifically, we want to see how player feedback from reviews can help to correlate to a price of a game. Does spending more money on a game cause a player to have a deeper impression of it and thus review it if they like/dislike it? Using data analysis tools with Python, pandas, and plotting libraries, we identify correlations that reveal how pricing impacts consumer behavior on the Steam digital marketplace.

column13: Impact of Gaming on Mental Health
column15: This project explores how machine learning can be applied to analyze and predict the effects of gaming behavior on mental health. Using a synthetic dataset modeled after real-world data on gaming habits, lifestyle factors, and demographic characteristics, the objective is to develop a classification model that accurately identifies an individual’s level of gaming addiction risk. The model will categorize users into four levels: Low, Moderate, High, and Severe, allowing for a more nuanced understanding of how gaming intensity may relate to mental health outcomes. By examining patterns such as time spent gaming, sleep habits, social interaction, and other behavioral indicators, the project aims to uncover meaningful relationships within the data. Additionally, this analysis demonstrates how predictive analytics can be used as an early detection tool to identify individuals who may be at higher risk. Ultimately, the project highlights the potential for machine learning to support mental health awareness, encourage healthier gaming behaviors, and contribute to preventative strategies in both clinical and everyday settings.
